html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Thirteen — Part Four

The Book of Shemu'el Bet

SHEMU'EL BET

The Second Book of Samuel

Chapters 19 through 24

Dawid's Return — Sheva's Rebellion — Dawid's Song — His Last Words — The Altar on Holy Ground

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

4QSama (4Q51) — Cave 4

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

SHEMU'EL BET — CHAPTERS 19 THROUGH 24 — DAWID'S RETURN AND CLOSING TESTAMENT

SHEMU'EL BET — CHAPTER 19 Dawid Returns to Yerushalayim — Shim'i Pardoned — Mefiboshet — Barzillai — Yisra'EL and Yehudah Quarrel

1 It was told Yoav, Behold, the king is weeping and mourning for Avshalom.

2 So the victory that day was turned into mourning for all the people, for the people heard that day, The king is grieving for his son.

3 And the people stole into the city that day as people who are ashamed steal away when they flee in battle.

4 The king covered his face, and the king cried with a loud voice, O my son Avshalom, O Avshalom, my son, my son.

5 Then Yoav came into the house to the king and said, You have today covered with shame the faces of all your servants, who have this day saved your life and the lives of your sons and your daughters and the lives of your wives and your concubines,

6 Because you love those who hate you and hate those who love you. For you have made it clear today that commanders and servants are nothing to you, for today I know that if Avshalom were alive and all of us were dead today, then you would be pleased.

7 Now therefore arise, go out and speak kindly to your servants, for I swear by YHWH, if you do not go, not a man will stay with you this night, and this will be worse for you than all the evil that has come upon you from your youth until now.

8 Then the king arose and took his seat in the gate. And the people were all told, Behold, the king is sitting in the gate. And all the people came before the king. Now Yisra'EL had fled every man to his own home.

9 And all the people were arguing throughout all the tribes of Yisra'EL, saying, The king delivered us from the hand of our enemies and saved us from the hand of the Pelishtim, and now he has fled out of the land from Avshalom.

10 But Avshalom, whom we anointed over us, has died in battle. Now therefore why do you say nothing about bringing the king back?

11 And King Dawid sent this message to Tzadok and Evyatar the priests, Say to the elders of Yehudah, Why should you be the last to bring the king back to his house, when the word of all Yisra'EL has come to the king?

12 You are my brothers — you are my bone and my flesh. Why then should you be the last to bring back the king?

13 And say to Amasa, Are you not my bone and my flesh? Creator do so to me, and more also, if you are not commander of my army from now on in place of Yoav.

14 And he swayed the heart of all the men of Yehudah as one man, so that they sent word to the king, Return, both you and all your servants.

15 So the king came back to the Yarden, and Yehudah came to Gilgal to meet the king and to bring the king over the Yarden.

16 And Shim'i the son of Gera, the Binyaminite, from Bakhurim, hurried to come down with the men of Yehudah to meet King Dawid.

17 And with him were a thousand men from Binyamin. And Tziva the servant of the house of Sha'ul, with his fifteen sons and his twenty servants, rushed down to the Yarden before the king,

18 And they crossed the ford to bring over the king's household and to do his pleasure. And Shim'i the son of Gera fell down before the king, as he was about to cross at the Yarden.

19 And he said to the king, Let not my lord hold me guilty or remember how your servant did wrong on the day my lord the king left Yerushalayim. Let not the king take it to heart.

20 For your servant knows that I have sinned. Therefore, behold, I have come this day, the first of all the house of Yosef to come down to meet my lord the king.

21 Avishai the son of Tzeruya answered, Shall not Shim'i be put to death for this, because he cursed YHWH's anointed?

22 But Dawid said, What have I to do with you, you sons of Tzeruya, that you should this day be as an adversary to me? Shall anyone be put to death in Yisra'EL this day? For do I not know that I am this day king over Yisra'EL?

23 And the king said to Shim'i, You shall not die. And the king gave him his oath.

24 And Mefiboshet the son of Sha'ul came down to meet the king. He had neither taken care of his feet nor trimmed his beard nor washed his clothes, from the day the king departed until the day he came back in safety.

25 And when he came to Yerushalayim to meet the king, the king said to him, Why did you not go with me, Mefiboshet?

26 He answered, My lord, O king, my servant deceived me, for your servant said, I will saddle a donkey for myself, that I may ride on it and go with the king. For your servant is lame.

27 He has slandered your servant to my lord the king. But my lord the king is like the angel of Creator — do therefore what is good in your eyes.

28 For all my father's house were but men doomed to death before my lord the king, but you set your servant among those who eat at your table. What further right have I, then, to cry to the king?

29 And the king said to him, Why speak any more of your affairs? I have decided: you and Tziva shall divide the land.

30 And Mefiboshet said to the king, Oh, let him take it all, since my lord the king has come safely home.

31 Now Barzillai the Gil'adite had come down from Rogelim, and he went on with the king to the Yarden, to escort him over the Yarden.

32 Barzillai was a very aged man, eighty years old. He had provided the king with food while he stayed at Makhanayim, for he was a very wealthy man.

33 And the king said to Barzillai, Come over with me, and I will provide for you with me in Yerushalayim.

34 But Barzillai said to the king, How many years have I still to live, that I should go up with the king to Yerushalayim?

35 I am this day eighty years old. Can I discern what is pleasant and what is not? Can your servant taste what he eats or what he drinks? Can I still listen to the voice of singing men and singing women? Why then should your servant be an added burden to my lord the king?

36 Your servant will go a little way over the Yarden with the king. Why should the king repay me with such a reward?

37 Please let your servant return, that I may die in my own city near the grave of my father and my mother. But here is your servant Kimham — let him go over with my lord the king, and do for him whatever seems good to you.

38 And the king answered, Kimham shall go over with me, and I will do for him whatever seems good to you, and all that you desire of me I will do for you.

39 Then all the people went over the Yarden, and the king went over. And the king kissed Barzillai and blessed him, and he returned to his own home.

40 The king went on to Gilgal, and Kimham went on with him. All the people of Yehudah, and also half the people of Yisra'EL, brought the king on his way.

41 Then all the men of Yisra'EL came to the king and said to the king, Why have our brothers the men of Yehudah stolen you away and brought the king and his household over the Yarden, and all Dawid's men with him?

42 All the men of Yehudah answered the men of Yisra'EL, Because the king is our close relative. Why then are you angry over this matter? Have we eaten at all at the king's expense? Or has he given us any gift?

43 And the men of Yisra'EL answered the men of Yehudah, We have ten shares in the king, and in Dawid also we have more than you. Why then did you despise us? Were we not the first to speak of bringing back our king? But the words of the men of Yehudah were fiercer than the words of the men of Yisra'EL.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 19. The covenant complexity of Dawid's return is fully on display: Yoav's rebuke, Shim'i's pardon, Mefiboshet's divided inheritance, Barzillai's refusal of royal honor, and the tribal quarrel between Yisra'EL and Yehudah that will eventually fracture the kingdom permanently.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 19 in full. Yoav's rebuke of Dawid — verse 5-7 — is one of the most direct confrontations of a king by a subordinate in the Nevi'im. Yoav is politically right: the king's public grief is destroying the army's morale. His covenant authority to say it is another matter.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 19 largely consistent with the Masoretic. Verse 30 — Mefiboshet said, let Tziva take it all, since my lord the king has come safely home — is the covenant declaration of Mefiboshet's true character: the land was never what mattered to him. The king's return is worth more than any inheritance.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 43 — the words of the men of Yehudah were fiercer than the words of the men of Yisra'EL. The tribal argument over who has more right to the king — Yehudah through kinship, Yisra'EL through numbers — is the covenant crack that will become the full schism after Shelomoh. The same fierce words will produce the permanent split in Melakhim Aleph 12. Dawid's return sows the seed of the kingdom's division by privileging one tribe over the other in the manner of his return.

SHEMU'EL BET — CHAPTER 20 Sheva's Rebellion — The Wise Woman of Avel — Yoav Kills Amasa — Dawid's Officials

1 Now there happened to be there a worthless man, whose name was Sheva, the son of Bikri, a Binyaminite. And he blew the trumpet and said, We have no portion in Dawid, and we have no inheritance in the son of Yishai — every man to his tents, O Yisra'EL.

2 So all the men of Yisra'EL withdrew from Dawid and followed Sheva the son of Bikri. But the men of Yehudah followed their king steadfastly from the Yarden to Yerushalayim.

3 And Dawid came to his house at Yerushalayim. And the king took the ten concubines whom he had left to care for the house and put them in a house under guard and provided for them, but did not go in to them. So they were shut up until the day of their death, living as if in widowhood.

4 Then the king said to Amasa, Call the men of Yehudah together to me within three days, and be here yourself.

5 So Amasa went to summon Yehudah, but he delayed beyond the set time that had been appointed him.

6 And Dawid said to Avishai, Now Sheva the son of Bikri will do us more harm than Avshalom. Take your lord's servants and pursue him, lest he get himself to fortified cities and escape from us.

7 And there went out after him Yoav's men and the Kerethites and the Pelethites, and all the mighty men. They went out from Yerushalayim to pursue Sheva the son of Bikri.

8 When they were at the great stone that is in Giv'on, Amasa came to meet them. Now Yoav was wearing a soldier's garment, and over it was a belt with a sword in its sheath fastened on his thigh, and as he went forward it fell out.

9 And Yoav said to Amasa, Is it well with you, my brother? And Yoav took Amasa by the beard with his right hand to kiss him.

10 But Amasa did not observe the sword that was in Yoav's hand. So Yoav struck him with it in the stomach and spilled his entrails to the ground without striking a second blow, and he died. Then Yoav and Avishai his brother pursued Sheva the son of Bikri.

11 And one of Yoav's young men stood by Amasa and said, Whoever favors Yoav, and whoever is for Dawid, let him follow Yoav.

12 And Amasa lay wallowing in his blood in the highway. And anyone who came by, seeing him, stopped. And when the man saw that all the people stopped, he carried Amasa out of the highway into the field and threw a garment over him.

13 When he was taken out of the highway, all the people went on after Yoav to pursue Sheva the son of Bikri.

14 And Sheva passed through all the tribes of Yisra'EL to Avel of Beit-Ma'akhah, and all the Bikrites assembled and followed him in.

15 And all the men who were with Yoav came and besieged him in Avel of Beit-Ma'akhah. They cast up a mound against the city, and it stood against the rampart, and they were battering the wall to throw it down.

16 Then a wise woman called from the city, Listen, listen. Tell Yoav, Come here, that I may speak to you.

17 And he came near her, and the woman said, Are you Yoav? He answered, I am. Then she said to him, Listen to the words of your servant. And he answered, I am listening.

18 Then she said, They used to say in former times, Let them but ask counsel at Avel, and so they settled a matter.

19 I am one of those who are peaceable and faithful in Yisra'EL. You seek to destroy a city that is a mother in Yisra'EL. Why will you swallow up the heritage of YHWH?

20 Yoav answered, Far be it from me, far be it, that I should swallow up or destroy.

21 That is not the case. But a man of the hill country of Efrayim, called Sheva the son of Bikri, has lifted his hand against King Dawid. Give up him alone, and I will withdraw from the city. And the woman said to Yoav, Behold, his head shall be thrown to you over the wall.

22 Then the woman went to all the people in her wisdom. And they cut off the head of Sheva the son of Bikri and threw it out to Yoav. So he blew the trumpet, and they dispersed from the city, every man to his home. And Yoav returned to Yerushalayim to the king.

23 Now Yoav was in command of all the army of Yisra'EL, and Benaya the son of Yehoyada was in command of the Kerethites and the Pelethites.

24 And Adoram was in charge of the forced labor, and Yehoshafat the son of Akhilud was the recorder.

25 And Sheva was secretary, and Tzadok and Evyatar were priests.

26 And Ira the Yairite was also Dawid's priest.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 20. Yoav's murder of Amasa — verses 9-10 — is the third man Yoav has killed in cold blood: Avner in Chapter 3, Avshalom in Chapter 18, and now Amasa. Each time he kills a man Dawid had specifically appointed or protected. Yoav consistently places his own covenant loyalty above Dawid's covenant authority.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 20 in full. The wise woman of Avel — verse 16-22 — is the covenant voice of wisdom that ends the rebellion with one beheading rather than the destruction of an entire city. She identifies the city as a mother in Yisra'EL — verse 19 — and the heritage of YHWH.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 20 largely consistent with the Masoretic. The administrative list at the end — verses 23-26 — mirrors the list in Chapter 8:16-18, showing the restoration of the covenant administrative order after the rebellion.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 19 — I am one of those who are peaceable and faithful in Yisra'EL — you seek to destroy a city that is a mother in Yisra'EL — why will you swallow up the heritage of YHWH? The wise woman's three-part question is the covenant advocacy of the vulnerable city against overwhelming military force. She identifies what is at stake: not merely buildings but the heritage of YHWH — covenant community, covenant memory, covenant life. Yoav recognizes the covenant claim and withdraws when the single guilty man is handed over. One head in exchange for a city.

SHEMU'EL BET — CHAPTER 21 The Famine and the Gibeonites — Ritzpah's Vigil — The Giants Slain — Dawid's Men Save the King

1 Now there was a famine in the days of Dawid for three years, year after year. And Dawid sought the face of YHWH. And YHWH said, There is bloodguilt on Sha'ul and on his house, because he put the Gibeonites to death.

2 So the king called the Gibeonites and spoke to them. Now the Gibeonites were not of the people of Yisra'EL but of the remnant of the Amorites. Although the people of Yisra'EL had sworn to spare them, Sha'ul had sought to strike them down in his zeal for the people of Yisra'EL and Yehudah.

3 And Dawid said to the Gibeonites, What shall I do for you? And how shall I make atonement, that you may bless the heritage of YHWH?

4 The Gibeonites said to him, It is not a matter of silver or gold between us and Sha'ul or his house — neither is it for us to put any man to death in Yisra'EL. He said, What do you say that I shall do for you?

5 They said to the king, The man who consumed us and planned to destroy us, so that we should have no place in all the territory of Yisra'EL,

6 Let seven of his sons be given to us, so that we may hang them before YHWH at Giv'ah of Sha'ul, the chosen of YHWH. And the king said, I will give them.

7 But the king spared Mefiboshet, the son of Sha'ul's son Yonatan, because of the oath of YHWH that was between them, between Dawid and Yonatan the son of Sha'ul.

8 The king took the two sons of Ritzpah the daughter of Ayah, whom she bore to Sha'ul, Armoni and Mefiboshet, and the five sons of Merav the daughter of Sha'ul, whom she bore to Adriel the son of Barzillai the Mekholathite.

9 And he gave them into the hands of the Gibeonites, and they hanged them on the mountain before YHWH, and the seven of them perished together. They were put to death in the first days of harvest, at the beginning of barley harvest.

10 Then Ritzpah the daughter of Ayah took sackcloth and spread it for herself on the rock, from the beginning of harvest until rain fell upon them from the heavens. And she did not allow the birds of the air to come upon them by day, or the wild animals by night.

11 When Dawid was told what Ritzpah the daughter of Ayah, the concubine of Sha'ul, had done,

12 Dawid went and took the bones of Sha'ul and the bones of his son Yonatan from the men of Yavesh-Gil'ad, who had stolen them from the public square of Beit-She'an, where the Pelishtim had hanged them, on the day the Pelishtim killed Sha'ul on Gilboa.

13 And he brought up from there the bones of Sha'ul and the bones of his son Yonatan, and they gathered the bones of those who were hanged.

14 And they buried the bones of Sha'ul and his son Yonatan in the land of Binyamin in Tzela, in the tomb of his father Kish. And they did all that the king commanded. And after that Creator responded to the plea for the land.

15 There was war again between the Pelishtim and Yisra'EL, and Dawid went down together with his servants, and they fought against the Pelishtim. And Dawid grew weary.

16 And Yishbi-Benov, one of the descendants of the giants, whose spear weighed three hundred shekels of bronze, and who was armed with a new sword, thought to kill Dawid.

17 But Avishai the son of Tzeruya came to his aid and attacked the Pelishtim and killed him. Then Dawid's men swore to him, You shall no longer go out with us to battle, lest you quench the lamp of Yisra'EL.

18 After this there was again war with the Pelishtim at Gov. Then Sibbekhai the Kushathite struck down Saf, who was one of the descendants of the giants.

19 And there was again war with the Pelishtim at Gov, and Elhanan the son of Ya'ir the Beit-Lekhemite struck down Golyat the Gittite, the shaft of whose spear was like a weaver's beam.

20 And there was again war at Gat, where there was a man of great stature, who had six fingers on each hand, and six toes on each foot, twenty-four in number, and he also was descended from the giants.

21 And when he taunted Yisra'EL, Yehonatan the son of Shim'ah, the brother of Dawid, struck him down.

22 These four were descended from the giants in Gat, and they fell by the hand of Dawid and by the hand of his servants.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 21. Ritzpah's vigil — verse 10 — guarding the bodies of her sons from birds and beasts from the beginning of harvest until the rains came — is one of the most haunting images of maternal covenant faithfulness in the Nevi'im. Her vigil moves Dawid to retrieve and properly bury the bones of Sha'ul and Yonatan.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 21 in full. Dawid's honoring of the covenant with the Gibeonites — despite the political cost of executing seven descendants of Sha'ul — and his protection of Mefiboshet — verse 7 — because of the oath of YHWH between Dawid and Yonatan — are read as the covenant principle that sworn oaths before YHWH bind generations.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 21 largely consistent with the Masoretic. Verse 17 — you shall no longer go out with us to battle, lest you quench the lamp of Yisra'EL — is the covenant declaration of Dawid's men acknowledging their king's irreplaceable covenant role. The lamp of Yisra'EL is the Davidic dynasty's covenant continuity.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10 — Ritzpah spread sackcloth on the rock and did not allow the birds of the air to come upon them by day or the wild animals by night. The woman who had no power to prevent the deaths of her sons exercises the only covenant act remaining to her: faithful presence at the place of death, protecting the bodies from desecration. Her covenant faithfulness, extended through the entire harvest season, moves the king to action — verse 11. Ritzpah's vigil accomplishes through covenant grief what no political argument could: the proper burial of Sha'ul and Yonatan, denied since the day of the battle on Gilboa.

DAWID'S SONG AND LAST WORDS — CHAPTERS 22 THROUGH 23

SHEMU'EL BET — CHAPTER 22 The Song of Dawid — YHWH Is My Rock — He Reached Down From on High — As the Light of Morning

1 And Dawid spoke to YHWH the words of this song on the day when YHWH delivered him from the hand of all his enemies, and from the hand of Sha'ul.

2 He said, YHWH is my rock and my fortress and my deliverer,

3 My Creator, my rock, in whom I take refuge, my shield, and the horn of my salvation, my stronghold and my refuge, my savior — you save me from violence.

4 I call upon YHWH, who is worthy to be praised, and I am saved from my enemies.

5 For the waves of death encompassed me, the torrents of destruction assailed me,

6 The cords of Sheol entangled me, the snares of death confronted me.

7 In my distress I called upon YHWH — to my Creator I called. From his temple he heard my voice, and my cry came to his ears.

8 Then the earth reeled and rocked, the foundations of the heavens trembled and quaked, because he was angry.

9 Smoke went up from his nostrils, and devouring fire from his mouth — glowing coals flamed forth from him.

10 He bowed the heavens and came down — thick darkness was under his feet.

11 He rode on a cherub and flew — he was seen on the wings of the wind.

12 He made darkness around him his canopy, thick clouds, a gathering of water.

13 Out of the brightness before him coals of fire flamed forth.

14 YHWH thundered from heaven, and the Most High uttered his voice.

15 And he sent out arrows and scattered them — lightning, and routed them.

16 Then the channels of the sea were seen, the foundations of the world were laid bare, at the rebuke of YHWH, at the blast of the breath of his nostrils.

17 He sent from on high, he took me — he drew me out of many waters.

18 He rescued me from my strong enemy, from those who hated me, for they were too mighty for me.

19 They confronted me in the day of my calamity, but YHWH was my support.

20 He brought me out into a broad place — he rescued me, because he delighted in me.

21 YHWH dealt with me according to my righteousness — according to the cleanness of my hands he rewarded me.

22 For I have kept the ways of YHWH and have not wickedly departed from my Creator.

23 For all his rules were before me, and from his statutes I did not turn aside.

24 I was blameless before him, and I kept myself from guilt.

25 And YHWH has rewarded me according to my righteousness, according to my cleanness in his sight.

26 With the merciful you show yourself merciful, with the blameless man you show yourself blameless,

27 With the purified you show yourself pure, and with the crooked you make yourself seem tortuous.

28 You save a humble people, but your eyes are on the haughty to bring them down.

29 For you are my lamp, O YHWH, and my Creator lightens my darkness.

30 For by you I can run against a troop, and by my Creator I can leap over a wall.

31 This Creator — his way is perfect — the word of YHWH proves true — he is a shield for all those who take refuge in him.

32 For who is Creator, except YHWH? And who is a rock, except our Creator?

33 This Creator is my strong refuge and has made my way blameless.

34 He made my feet like the feet of a deer and set me secure on the heights.

35 He trains my hands for war, so that my arms can bend a bow of bronze.

36 You have given me the shield of your salvation, and your gentleness made me great.

37 You gave a wide place for my steps under me, and my feet did not slip.

38 I pursued my enemies and destroyed them, and did not turn back until they were consumed.

39 I consumed them — I thrust them through, so that they did not rise — they fell under my feet.

40 For you equipped me with strength for the battle — you made those who rise against me sink under me.

41 You made my enemies turn their backs to me, those who hated me, and I destroyed them.

42 They looked, but there was none to save — they cried to YHWH, but he did not answer them.

43 I beat them fine as the dust of the earth — I crushed them and stamped them down like the mire of the streets.

44 You delivered me from strife with my people — you kept me as the head of the nations — people whom I had not known served me.

45 Foreigners came cringing to me — as soon as they heard of me, they obeyed me.

46 Foreigners lost heart and came trembling out of their fortresses.

47 YHWH lives, and blessed be my rock, and exalted be my Creator, the rock of my salvation,

48 The Creator who gave me vengeance and brought down peoples under me,

49 Who brought me out from my enemies — you exalted me above those who rose against me — you delivered me from men of violence.

50 For this I will praise you, O YHWH, among the nations, and sing praises to your name.

51 Great salvation he brings to his king, and shows steadfast love to his anointed, to Dawid and his offspring forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 22. The Song of Dawid is preserved with minor variants in Psalm 18. The DSS Psalms scroll (11QPsa) and the 4QSam manuscripts together provide the most complete early witness to the Dawid song tradition. Verse 51 — steadfast love to his anointed, to Dawid and his offspring forever — is the covenant summary of the Nathan oracle in poetic form.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 22 in full. The theophany of verses 8-16 — the earth reeling, the heavens bowing, YHWH riding on a cherub through fire and darkness — is read as the covenant declaration of YHWH's personal intervention in Dawid's deliverance. The cosmic scale of the imagery matches the covenant weight of the deliverance.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic preserves Chapter 22 as largely identical to Psalm 18, with minor variants. The song's placement here — near the end of Dawid's life — positions it as the covenant testimony of a completed career: the shepherd boy who faced Golyat now sings of deliverance from all his enemies.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 26-28 — with the merciful you show yourself merciful — with the blameless you show yourself blameless — with the crooked you make yourself seem tortuous — you save a humble people but your eyes are on the haughty to bring them down. The covenant theology of YHWH's character is presented in relational terms: YHWH responds to the character of those who approach him. This is not Dawid claiming perfect blamelessness — the narrative of Bathsheva and Uriyah has already been told. It is the covenant observation that YHWH's disposition toward a person reflects the person's fundamental covenant orientation over a lifetime, not individual failures.

SHEMU'EL BET — CHAPTER 23 The Last Words of Dawid — His Mighty Men — The Three and the Thirty

1 Now these are the last words of Dawid: The oracle of Dawid, the son of Yishai, the oracle of the man who was raised on high, the anointed of the Creator of Yaakov, the sweet psalmist of Yisra'EL:

2 The Spirit of YHWH speaks by me — his word is on my tongue.

3 The Creator of Yisra'EL has spoken — the Rock of Yisra'EL has said to me: When one rules justly over men, ruling in the fear of Creator,

4 He dawns on them like the morning light, like the sun shining forth on a cloudless morning, like rain that makes grass sprout from the earth.

5 For does not my house stand so with Creator? For he has made with me an everlasting covenant, ordered in all things and secure. For will he not cause to prosper all my help and my desire?

6 But worthless men are all like thorns that are thrown away, for they cannot be taken with the hand.

7 But the man who touches them arms himself with iron and the shaft of a spear, and they are utterly consumed with fire in their place.

8 These are the names of the mighty men whom Dawid had: Yoseb-Bassheveth, a Takhkemonite — he was chief of the three. He wielded his spear against eight hundred whom he killed at one time.

9 And next to him among the three mighty men was Ele'azar the son of Dodo, son of Akhohi. He was with Dawid when they defied the Pelishtim who were gathered there for battle, and the men of Yisra'EL withdrew.

10 He rose and struck down the Pelishtim until his hand was weary, and his hand clung to the sword. And YHWH brought about a great victory that day, and the men returned after him only to strip the slain.

11 And next to him was Shammah, the son of Agee the Khararite. The Pelishtim gathered together at Lehi, where there was a plot of ground full of lentils, and the men fled from the Pelishtim.

12 But he took his stand in the midst of the plot and defended it and struck down the Pelishtim, and YHWH worked a great victory.

13 And three of the thirty chief men went down and came about harvest time to Dawid at the cave of Adullam, when a band of Pelishtim was encamped in the Valley of Refa'im.

14 Dawid was then in the stronghold, and the garrison of the Pelishtim was then at Beit-Lekhem.

15 And Dawid said longingly, Oh, that someone would give me water to drink from the well of Beit-Lekhem that is by the gate.

16 Then the three mighty men broke through the camp of the Pelishtim and drew water out of the well of Beit-Lekhem that was by the gate and carried and brought it to Dawid. But he would not drink of it. He poured it out to YHWH

17 And said, Far be it from me, O YHWH, that I should do this. Shall I drink the blood of the men who went at the risk of their lives? Therefore he would not drink it. These things the three mighty men did.

18 Now Avishai, the brother of Yoav, the son of Tzeruya, was chief of the thirty. And he wielded his spear against three hundred men and killed them and won a name beside the three.

19 He was the most renowned of the thirty and became their commander, but he did not attain to the three.

20 And Benaya the son of Yehoyada was a valiant man of Kavtze'el, a doer of great deeds. He struck down two heroes of Moav. He also went down and struck down a lion in a pit on a day when snow had fallen.

21 And he struck down an Egyptian, a handsome man. The Egyptian had a spear in his hand, but Benaya went down to him with a staff and snatched the spear out of the Egyptian's hand and killed him with his own spear.

22 These things did Benaya the son of Yehoyada, and won a name beside the three mighty men.

23 He was renowned among the thirty, but he did not attain to the three. And Dawid set him over his bodyguard.

24 Asah'el the brother of Yoav was one of the thirty. Elhanan the son of Dodo of Beit-Lekhem,

25 Shammah of Kharod, Elika of Kharod,

26 Khelez the Paltite, Ira the son of Ikkesh of Teko'a,

27 Aviezer of Anatot, Mevunnai the Kushathite,

28 Tzalmon the Akhohite, Maharai of Netofah,

29 Khelev the son of Ba'anah of Netofah, Ittai the son of Rivai of Giv'ah of Binyamin,

30 Benaya of Piraton, Hiddai of the brooks of Gaash,

31 Avi-Albon the Arvathite, Azmaveth of Barkhum,

32 Elyakhva the Sha'albonite, the sons of Yashen, Yonatan,

33 Shammah the Khararite, Akhiam the son of Sharar the Urazite,

34 Eliefelet the son of Ahashai of Maakhah, Eliam the son of Akhitofel the Gilonite,

35 Khetzrai the Karmelite, Pa'arai the Arvite,

36 Yig'al the son of Natan of Tzovah, Bani the Gadite,

37 Tzelek the Ammonite, Nakhrai the Beerothite, the armor-bearer of Yoav the son of Tzeruya,

38 Ira the Yithrite, Garev the Yithrite,

39 Uriyah the Khittite — thirty-seven in all.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 23. The last words of Dawid — verses 1-7 — are a covenant testament: the Spirit of YHWH speaks by me — his word is on my tongue. Dawid identifies himself as the instrument of YHWH's prophetic word, not merely a military and political leader. The covenant king is also a covenant prophet.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 23 in full. Verse 16-17 — Dawid pours out the water from Beit-Lekhem before YHWH — is read as the covenant act of transforming personal longing into covenant worship. Three men risked death to bring him what he desired. He consecrates it to YHWH rather than consuming it.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 23 largely consistent with the Masoretic. The list of the thirty mighty men ends with Uriyah the Khittite — verse 39. The narrative places the man Dawid had murdered at the end of the honor roll of Dawid's greatest warriors. The text does not comment on this. It does not need to.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 39 — Uriyah the Khittite — thirty-seven in all. The list of Dawid's covenant mighty men — the men who gave their lives for his kingdom — closes with the name of the man Dawid murdered. The placement is not accidental. Uriyah was one of the thirty-seven covenant champions who served Dawid faithfully. His death by Dawid's order is the permanent covenant stain that the narrative of Chapter 11 established and that verse 39 quietly confirms. The covenant king who sang of his own righteousness in Chapter 22 has Uriyah's name at the end of his honor roll.

SHEMU'EL BET — CHAPTER 24 The Census — Dawid's Sin — The Plague — The Threshing Floor of Araunah — An Altar to YHWH

1 Again the anger of YHWH was kindled against Yisra'EL, and he incited Dawid against them, saying, Go, number Yisra'EL and Yehudah.

2 So the king said to Yoav, the commander of the army, who was with him, Go through all the tribes of Yisra'EL, from Dan to Be'er-Sheva, and number the people, that I may know the number of the people.

3 But Yoav said to the king, May YHWH your Creator add to the people a hundred times as many as they are, while the eyes of my lord the king still see it. But why does my lord the king delight in this thing?

4 But the king's word prevailed against Yoav and the commanders of the army. So Yoav and the commanders of the army went out from the presence of the king to number the people of Yisra'EL.

5 They crossed the Yarden and began from Aro'er, and from the city that is in the middle of the valley, toward Gad and on to Ya'zer.

6 Then they came to Gil'ad, and to Kadesh in the land of the Khittites, and they came to Dan, and from Dan they went around to Tzidon

7 And came to the fortress of Tzor and to all the cities of the Khivvites and Kena'anites, and they went out to the Negev of Yehudah at Be'er-Sheva.

8 So when they had gone through all the land, they came to Yerushalayim at the end of nine months and twenty days.

9 And Yoav gave the sum of the numbering of the people to the king: in Yisra'EL there were eight hundred thousand valiant men who drew the sword, and the men of Yehudah were five hundred thousand.

10 But Dawid's heart struck him after he had numbered the people. And Dawid said to YHWH, I have sinned greatly in what I have done. But now, O YHWH, please take away the iniquity of your servant, for I have done very foolishly.

11 And when Dawid arose in the morning, the word of YHWH came to the prophet Gad, Dawid's seer, saying,

12 Go and say to Dawid, Thus says YHWH, Three things I offer you. Choose one of them, that I may do it to you.

13 So Gad came to Dawid and told him, and said to him, Shall three years of famine come to you in your land? Or will you flee three months before your foes while they pursue you? Or shall there be three days' pestilence in your land? Now consider, and decide what answer I shall return to him who sent me.

14 Then Dawid said to Gad, I am in great distress. Let us fall into the hand of YHWH, for his mercy is great, but let me not fall into the hand of man.

15 So YHWH sent a pestilence on Yisra'EL from the morning until the appointed time. And there died of the people from Dan to Be'er-Sheva seventy thousand men.

16 And when the angel stretched out his hand toward Yerushalayim to destroy it, YHWH relented from the calamity and said to the angel who was working destruction among the people, It is enough. Now stay your hand. And the angel of YHWH was by the threshing floor of Araunah the Yebusite.

17 Then Dawid spoke to YHWH when he saw the angel who was striking the people, and said, Behold, I have sinned, and I have done wickedly. But these sheep, what have they done? Please let your hand be against me and against my father's house.

18 And Gad came to Dawid that day and said to him, Go up, raise an altar to YHWH on the threshing floor of Araunah the Yebusite.

19 So Dawid went up at Gad's word, as YHWH commanded.

20 And when Araunah looked down, he saw the king and his servants coming on toward him. And Araunah went out and paid homage to the king with his face to the ground.

21 And Araunah said, Why has my lord the king come to his servant? Dawid said, To buy the threshing floor from you, in order to build an altar to YHWH, that the plague may be averted from the people.

22 Then Araunah said to Dawid, Let my lord the king take and offer up what seems good to him. Here are the oxen for the burnt offering and the threshing sledges and the yokes of the oxen for the wood.

23 All this, O king, Araunah gives to the king. And Araunah said to the king, May YHWH your Creator accept you.

24 But the king said to Araunah, No, but I will buy it from you for a price. I will not offer burnt offerings to YHWH my Creator that cost me nothing. So Dawid bought the threshing floor and the oxen for fifty shekels of silver.

25 And Dawid built there an altar to YHWH and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings. So YHWH responded to the plea for the land, and the plague was averted from Yisra'EL.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 24. The census and its consequence — the plague that kills seventy thousand — closes Shemu'el Bet. The threshing floor of Araunah where Dawid builds the altar is identified in Divrei HaYamim as the site where the temple of Shelomoh will be built. The covenant act of purchase and sacrifice establishes the holy ground for the covenant house that Dawid was not permitted to build.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 24 in full. Verse 14 — let us fall into the hand of YHWH, for his mercy is great, but let me not fall into the hand of man — is read as the covenant declaration of the principle that YHWH's harshest discipline is preferable to human vengeance. Dawid chooses the pestilence not from masochism but from covenant trust in YHWH's mercy.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 24 largely consistent with the Masoretic. Verse 24 — I will not offer burnt offerings to YHWH my Creator that cost me nothing — is one of the defining covenant declarations of the entire Dawid narrative. The covenant king refuses the gift that would make his worship costless. Worship without sacrifice is not worship.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 17 — Dawid spoke to YHWH when he saw the angel striking the people: behold, I have sinned — these sheep, what have they done? Let your hand be against me and against my father's house. The intercession for the people — made at the moment of seeing the consequence of his own sin falling on them — is the covenant act that ends the plague. Dawid identifies the injustice of the innocent suffering for his guilt and offers himself as the alternative. YHWH responds. Verse 24 — I will not offer burnt offerings that cost me nothing. Shemu'el Bet ends with this declaration. The covenant king purchases the holy ground, builds the altar, and offers the sacrifice at personal cost. This is the covenant ground on which the temple will stand.

END OF PART FOUR — CHAPTERS 19 THROUGH 24 — SHEMU'EL BET COMPLETE
